person_name: George of Cappadocia, Arian Bishop of Alexandria (d. 361)
person_name_full: George of Cappadocia, Arian Bishop of Alexandria (d. 361)
notes: Arian bishop of Alexandria, known for his opposition to Athanasius and controversial tenure.

docs.title: BOOK XXII
docs.author: Ammianus Marcellinus
docs.excerpt: 7. To these wicked suggestions he added this also, which soon afterwards led to his destruction. As he was returning from court, and passing by the superb temple of the Genius, escorted by a large train, as was his custom, he turned his eyes towards the temple, and

docs.title: Letter 56
docs.author: Athanasius of Alexandria
docs.subject_summary: Copy of a letter of the Emperor Jovian, sent to Athanasius, the most holy Archbishop of Alexandria:
docs.excerpt: … of Cappadocia, the remains of that unholy George who desolated the city and the world.' The Emperor on hearing this set spurs to his horse, and departed to the Camp. Second Petition of the Arians.

docs.title: Letter 67
docs.author: Julian the Apostate
docs.subject_summary: "Thou hast come, Telemachus!" 6 as the verse says, but in your letters I have already seen you and
docs.excerpt: … already seen you and the image of your noble soul — like an imposing design stamped on a small seal. Much can be revealed in little. Pheidias, the great sculptor, was not famous only for his colossal statues at Olympia and Athens. He also knew how to confine masterwork within tiny dimensions.
